source: CIS AZURE KUBERNETES SERVICE (AKS) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF_updated_20250825_190647.json

input_row.title: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
input_row.description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
input_row.rationale: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs. Impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
input_row.audit: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
input_row.remediation: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables. Default Value: By default, secrets are not defined References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v2- identity-management#im-7-eliminate-unintended-credential-exposure Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
input_row.impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
input_row.reference: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v2- identity-management#im-7-eliminate-unintended-credential-exposure Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod

gpt_response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
The control can be verified automatically because:
- The audit mentions a command (`kubectl get all`) to check environment variable usage, indicating that it can be scripted and automated.
- This command can be executed in an automated fashion using an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) context without the need for manual intervention or human judgment. 

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_aks_secrets_preference_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check:
- Use the `kubectl` command line tool to connect to the Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster and execute the following command:
  ```bash
  kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
  ```
- Specific resources to enumerate: All Kubernetes resources across all namespaces within the AKS cluster.
  
- Verify that the output of the command does not list any objects that utilize environment variables defined from secrets. 

**Clear Pass/Fail criteria:**
- **Pass:** The command output is empty, indicating that no Kubernetes objects are using secrets as environment variables.
- **Fail:** The command output lists any Kubernetes objects that use secrets as environment variables.

- Example API endpoint or CLI command to validate this would be the `kubectl` command specified above, used in the context of an AKS cluster environment where the user has sufficient permissions to query resources.
